dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Breathing Problems, 58-year-old female at [CITY], [STREET_ADDRESS] with reported chest pain and shortness of breath.
Hi, I'm Heather, an EMT with the rescue squad. What caused you to call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing and chest pain, and it just got really bad over the last hour.
What is your name?
My name is Marly.
And can you tell me the day of the week and the month?
Today is Saturday, and the month is May.
We need to assess your airway. Your airway appears to be open and unobstructed.
Your airway is clear and patent, and there are no signs of swelling or obstruction. Is that right?
May we check your breathing now? Your respirations seem rapid.
Resp is 25 and labored. You seem to be struggling.
Yes, it's hard to catch my breath and hard to talk.
Our pulse oximeter shows an oxygen saturation of 90%. I'm putting a nasal cannula on you. Okay with that?
Pulse is 81 and blood pressure is 185 over 91. No bleeding. Skin is warm and dry. Let's check your pulse now.
When did you first notice the shortness of breath and chest pain?
I woke up about an hour ago. I have asthma, COPD, heart failure, high blood pressure, and chronic kidney disease.
You have a documented allergy to penicillin and tape. Any other allergies?
I take Minoxidil, Labetalol, Clonidine, ProAir, Albuterol, and Lorazepam.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
I believe I drank coffee before going to bed, but I was asleep for the last hour when this started.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the chest pain you're experiencing?
It's an 8. It feels like pressure.
We're now going to perform a head and neck exam to determine if there's any issue with the trachea or neck position.
You appear alert and oriented. Can you confirm today is Saturday and that we're in [CITY]?
Yes, it's Saturday and we're in [CITY]. I do feel a bit sluggish.
I've noticed some bilateral pitting edema in your feet and your skin is pink, warm, and dry. Let's take a closer look.
Can I check your lungs with a stethoscope? Just take a deep breath in.
Breath sounds are diminished bilaterally but equal and not reactive. Any chest pain on movement or breathing?
Pulse is 81.
Respirations are 25 initially.
Blood pressure is 185 over 91.
Glucose measurement is not available with our portable device.
Oxygen saturation at 90% currently.
The first 4-lead EKG was taken and will be followed up with a 12-lead for more details.
We're going to start monitoring your capnography with a nasal cannula to help track breathing. Ready for that?
I'll now apply 1 inch of transdermal nitroglycerin paste to the right side of your chest.
We must start an IV in your right hand to prepare for any additional medications, if that's okay with you?
Based on your symptoms and history, we'll be following the respiratory distress protocol for COPD/asthma and a chest pain for potential cardiac cause.
Pulse is 83 now.
IV access has been successfully established in your right hand.
How is the pressure in your chest now after the medication?
Are there any other symptoms such as dizziness or nausea you want to report?
No, just the pressure and the difficulty breathing. I noticed my feet are a little less swollen now.
Blood pressure is 177 over 87 and oxygen is still at 97%.
The 12-lead EKG came back as sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST and T wave changes.
You stated you want to go to [COMPANY_NAME], that is correct?
We'll help you move from the couch to the stretcher now. It will be a smooth and gentle transfer.
You're now secured on the stretcher. We'll maintain your safety and make sure you're comfortable during the ride.
We're set to transport you to the hospital and hand you over to their emergency team.
The hospital team will need to evaluate your heart further and closely monitor your breathing as well.
We may need to check your oxygen or blood pressure again during the trip. Is that okay with you?
I'll periodically monitor your pulse and breathing and give you updates.
Your pulse remains steady at 82, which is stable. You're responding well to the care.
Upon arrival, we'll give them a detailed background and all treatment actions taken so far.
Once you're off the stretcher, their team will take complete care of your condition.
We are now beginning transport and will keep closely monitoring your breathing and oxygen levels.
No signs of tracheal deviation observed.
I see you have a history of cocaine abuse. Have you used cocaine recently?
I've used in the past, but not recently.
You had some very high breathing at first-25 to 28. Have you ever experienced this severe before?
Also, for medical records, you have osteoarthritis. Does it affect your daily mobility?
It does sometimes, especially long walks.
You continue to be alert and oriented. How are you feeling now compared to when we first saw you?
I feel still a bit weak but my breathing is easier now.
Respirations are now about 28. That's still high, but we're on track to stabilize it.
Your airway remains clear with no obstruction.
Your eyes fully open upon verbal stimulation-no difficulty.
Can you follow a simple instruction? Touch both your ears with your fingers. Looks normal.
Skin remains pink, warm, and dry with normal turgor. You have edema, but no rash or discoloration.
The first EKG wasn't stable to perform, but now the 4-lead and 12-lead readings are good.
You are now secured. Any arm movement or discomfort to report?
Our destination remains [COMPANY_NAME] as you requested.
Just confirming, was the toe swelling a recent problem or long-term?
The swelling is long-term, due to my heart failure.
BP is at 177 over 87, and oxygen remains at 97%.
We're continuing at 10-15 LPM of oxygen with clear signs of improvement.
IV is in place and ready for medication or fluids. Respiratory and EKG signs are trending in the right direction.
We will inform the hospital team about your positive reaction to nitroglycerin.
GCS score is 15-fully alert and oriented. That's an important note.
Tracheal position is normal, as you have no signs of deviation or jugular venous distension.
Known allergy to tape. Please avoid adhesive materials for dressing or bandage contact.
Final check in-your arms are held comfortable. You're ready to go.
We'll assist you using the sheet-pull method for positioning and safety in the hospital bed.
Your vitals are stable and will continue to be monitored to or arrival.
All care steps were successfully performed and any changes were calmly addressed.
We'll submit a full report to the ED team with signed details.
That concludes our care and transfer of the patient. Thank you for your support.
Your EKG did show nonspecific ST and T wave changes. Our staff will pass this along to the hospital team.
Your history includes anxiety. This may be further considered by the hospital team when assessing further.
